speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Rena Zabielak. We're in London, England. And this is tape number five/

speaker: interviewee
text: But he was a prosecutor late in Nuremberg by [NON-ENGLISH]. And I worked with him there with UNRRA. And we were distributing food to people because we got-- lots of food was arriving.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was September '44?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was September '44. It was nearly the end of September. And I was working with him. And I told him that he is Jewish. He said, no, he's not Jewish. I said, I am Jewish, even that my name is Maria [NON-ENGLISH]. Then I saw what was going on there in Warsaw. And I was scared that if the Germans didn't kill me, the Poles will kill me. Even that I had the big responsible job, I left it. And I went to Otwock outside Warsaw. I came to Otwock. And I met a young lady what I knew. I didn't know her. But I knew the family. And I said, let us live together.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish, yes, she also. And her husband was in Warsaw in the ruins, because Warsaw was bombed and burned. And I forgot to mention that while I was there, there was the uprising in Warsaw. And my mother and my husband were there in the ruins. I didn't know what happened to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your other uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was with her there. She-- and she took me to a family what she knew. We went from Otwock to Lublin. And Lublin was, at the time, the capital of Warsaw. And there was a man, Davidson, who used-- what her family knew very well-- actually, her sister. And he used to be in the film business. And this Mr. Davidson gave us a job in [NON-ENGLISH]. It was a Russian firm. And I was a cashier. And she was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a chairman. You know, in a shop. She was like a manageress. And this Mr. Davidson found us a flat. And I had some money on me. And I paid $100. He gave us a flat. And we lived together in a flat in-- in Lublin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your money was in dollars?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had some dollars on me. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And one day-- but she wasn't a very nice person. You see, when we worked together, we used to come in the evening. But she had some friends that she used to leave me on a Saturday, on a Sunday. And I used to walk on the streets and cry. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I didn't know what happened to my family. And I was very, very concerned. But first, when we arrived, she took me to a family and say, give us, again, some-- a sack of straw. And we slept there. And I had a fur coat from my husband. And we covered up with that fur coat. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when Warsaw was taken, then-- Warsaw was taken by the Russians-- because they stopped for six months. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Warsaw was taken by the Russians. And I was in Lublin. And that brother of the chief of police, who was also on the police, was going to Warsaw. And I said I will go with him. I went with him to Warsaw. And I went to the place where my mother and Arnold supposed to be.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was an old-- what is the woman? Outside Warsaw-- it was about 15 kilometers from Warsaw. And I walked there. And I came to the woman. And she said to me, your mother is for sure dead. Who was this woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: That [NON-ENGLISH] what-- but Arnold and [NON-ENGLISH] were sent as Poles to Germany. They are probably alive. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] What shall I tell you? I wanted to see if she's saying truth or a lie. And I took her [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to somebody who says from cards, to a-- how is it called? To a fortune teller.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I took her to a fortune teller. And I watched her face, what she will say. But you couldn't see anything in her face because she was a very wicked woman. And I walked back to Warsaw crying.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came to that woman, what Yanka was in hiding. And I knew that my husband hid there some money under the floor-- under the floor in the basement. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But she said she hasn't got nothing to eat. And as I had a permission-- you know, I had a-- how is it? Permit?

speaker: interviewee
text: A permit to be able to go in from Warsaw and out of Warsaw. Then I went through the Vistula. The Vistula was frozen. And I brought him some vodka, some sausage, some bread. And we had, in the evening, some food. And I employed somebody to try to take me out the money from under the floor. And as the man was doing this, my mother walked in. My mother walked in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she know you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she knew about this Polish woman, where she is. My mother walked in. And when my mother walked in, I first wanted to take out the money. We took out the money. I paid them. And I went back again to that chief of police, to that family. And they gave me the flat what I was in hiding with those women.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the reunion with you and your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you imagine? It was something so moving that you can't imagine. And as I went there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they gave us this room. But it had a bed. And the bed didn't have a mattress, only the springs. My mother had a blanket. Then we put on the blanket on the springs. And with the coats we covered ourselves. And we had there a little stove. And it was winter. It was cold. It was January. And then we made a little fire. And I went out. And I used to bring some food. But my mother was ill. And I had to look after her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she know what had happened to your two uncles?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she didn't, because they were separated when they left Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the little girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: The little girl, I knew she's all right because this woman told me that she's all right. And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I decided to make a business with a man to go to Lublin and to start doing some business to earn some money. I was still young. But you need money to live, because this money what we had wasn't enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you must have used up quite a lot over that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I went-- (WHISPERING) oh, my god. I went to-- With that man, we went to Lublin by train. It took 48 hours-- it was only 60 kilometers-- for the train to come from Grochow to Lublin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did it take so long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because every time, it broke down something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we arrived there, we were there about two hours. And the man came. And he told me that Arnold is back in Warsaw, there in Grochow with my mother. And Jurek, the-- the office-- this minister's brother was going to Warsaw. And I went with him to Warsaw. I came to Warsaw. When I saw Arnold, I will never forget that moment. And then he went-- then this woman brought Yanka. And [NON-ENGLISH] came there as well. And we had to find some accommodation for us. And Arnold went to Lodz. And he found a flat. And we all moved to Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was early in 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it easy to move around, because the war hadn't finished?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was still-- it was 1945. It was easy. That time, we were free.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was in the early years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Early in the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We went to Lodz. We had a flat. And I opened a business in Lodz. And Arnold had a different business.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of business did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a wholesale of hosiery. And I had some partners.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it difficult to get the hosiery?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was-- you know, it was from hand to hand. It was-- [DRINKS]. One day-- And I sent a letter when I was liberated to Arnold's brother, to England. And I don't know how it was coming-- a minister from England. And one evening we were coming back home. And somebody was knocking-- because in Poland, there is-- they close the gates-- at the gate. And he said he is looking for us. He came from London from his brother. He brought a ring for him. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were in Lodz. And on the beginning of '46, Arnold, with Sylvia, went to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sylvia was the little girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was now back to using her real name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Name.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened between the spring of '45 and the beginning of '46? You were working in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Then in 1946, on the 18th of September, to the two years, I landed in England.

speaker: interviewer
text: And until then, you just stayed in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: With your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my mother. And I had a business there. The business, those partners twisted me left, right, and center. They took me off. But I wanted to leave Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they Jewish partners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And I came to England. And Arnold was staying with one brother, because he had two brothers here. And I was staying with the other brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother come with you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother came six months later. And when Mommy arrived-- in the meantime, we bought a house in Holly Park over the road. And when Mommy arrived, a week later, we got married. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I straight away had a family. I had a husband, a mother, and a daughter-- step-daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was she then, the step-daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was 11 years difference between the two of us. And we got married. And the following year, Henry was born, in 1948. Sylvia was going to Hasmonean Grammar School. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was a housewife. And Arnold didn't have permission to work. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When Henry was two, I took him to the Home Office. We didn't have-- they told us to leave. Then he went with me to the Home Office. And he said, don't throw my parents out. I am British. And funny enough, Arnold got permission to stay in England.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was Henry at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years. [INTERVIEWER LAUGHING]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you told him what to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Sylvia went to Hasmonean Grammar School. And I want her to be a chemist, a pharmacist. She said she must earn money. And she left Hasmonean. She made full all levels. And she went to learn shorthand typist. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And she got a job. And she was earning 5 pounds a week. And my husband used to take the mickey out of me that I will never be able to earn money. And this upset me. And when Henry was about four and a half, and he had a business, a wholesale of fancy jewelry, I went to work there. And they paid me 5 pounds a week. Swell. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: When Sylvia was 18, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one day in the morning, Arnold comes in that he had a letter from the register that she wants to get married. And she wants to get married to somebody. And Arnold went. And he said, what is this? She said somebody played a trick on us. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to the office. And I told this to the girls who worked with me in the office. And they said, there isn't a trick like this with the register. Let Mr. Arnold ring up to find out. And we found out that Sylvia really wants to get married to somebody who is not Jewish what she met in work. And Arnold rang up to tell her. My mother went out shopping. And she ran away. And she ran away with him to Scotland. We followed her to Scotland. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And what was going on there was terrible. We had a terrible row. And Arnold said he doesn't want to see her evermore in his life. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But he was terribly upset. And when he came home, he knocked his head on the wall. And I said, look, Arnold, she left you. But I will never leave you. I love you too much. But he lost all the money. He lost his head by losing her. And we lost all the friends because he was ashamed to look somebody in the face because all the papers were writing about.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I opened a little shop in [NON-ENGLISH] selling handbags and jewelry. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then the Germans start paying compensation. Then I told myself, why shouldn't she get compensation? Then I went to the solicitor. I never told my husband anything. And I told him to write a letter to her in Lodz. I had the address. What shall I tell you? After five years, I brought her back home. And she never had children. But she did marry this man?

speaker: interviewee
text: She married this man.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say she came back without him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, with him. I-- first, I had to go to her. The way how I found it was a whole-- you know, it is very complicated. Then, she-- you know, she didn't have children. I had a business in [NON-ENGLISH]. And then I had another business, because the shop was-- I had to close. The lease expired. And I opened another shop. And I found out that my mother got cancer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year are you talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: '62. I found out that my mother got cancer. Then I said, I've got to give up a business-- no. When Henry was bar mitzvah in '61, they paid me out very well for the other shop what I had in [NON-ENGLISH]. And for a year I was doing nothing. And then I took a shop in [NON-ENGLISH]. When I took the shop in [NON-ENGLISH] before I opened I had trouble already. Henry cut his tendon. And then I found out that Mommy got cancer. Then I decided to get rid of the shop and to look after my mother. And then I bought a property to be able to have an income, because one person couldn't, you know, keep this standard of living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your husband was working though?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and he had another business. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a reconciliation between your husband and his daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a reconciliation. But they were never--

speaker: interviewer
text: --the same.

speaker: interviewee
text: --same. And she didn't have children. When Mommy had cancer, she came to me. And she said, you know, what? I can't have children. I'm going to adopt. Then I said, don't take a boy. Take a girl. It won't be so complicated. And she took a little girl. And one day, when Mommy was very ill-- she was in the hospital, in Finchley Memorial Hospital in a private room. She-- it was Mommy's birthday. Then I collected her with the baby. And she put the baby on the bed. And the baby was a few months old. And I said, Sylvia, she will fall down. Why don't you-- she said, she doesn't move. And I was really upset. I didn't know what to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, two days later, I rang up there. And her husband answered the telephone. I said, what you are doing here?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, I'll tell you what it is. The baby, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we took her to the hospital. And we found out that she got a lump on her brain. And they are going to make a lumbar puncture. And then they took the baby to the hospital. And they operated. And they took out a cyst as big as a grapefruit pressing on the brain. Then my husband said to her, you better leave this baby, because you will only have trouble. She started screaming, shouting, this is my baby! But it was first-- it still was on fostering. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Then my mother died. My mother died. She still had the baby. And then they came from the council. And they said, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] listen, this baby is not suitable for adoption. And they took her away. And every week, they used to go to look after the baby. One day she rings me up. And she says, they want me to take her back. In the meantime, she got a very good job. And she worked. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then she came over here without her husband. And I turned around like a witch. And I said, Sylvia, perhaps one day you will have your own baby. Don't worry. And she got pregnant. And she had a little boy. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] That was after my mother died. It was everything OK. She had-- a few years later she had a little boy. And then my husband got ill. My husband got ill. The carotid artery sent emblems to the brain.
